title: Automate Your Workflow
tagline: Take a repetitive task you are tired of. Build an automation that handles it. Save hours every week.
outcome: When you finish this project you will have a working automation for a real task you do every week. You will have a master prompt you can reuse forever, a workflow map, and a framework that transfers to every future automation you build.
submission_prompt: Describe in one or two sentences what your automation does and roughly how much time it saves per week. Optionally paste a link to your master prompt, a gist, or a screenshot.

steps.title: Pick a task worth automating
steps.body:
Every week you do something that follows the same pattern. Something repetitive, predictable, and time-consuming. That is your target.

A great first automation hits four tests: you can explain it in under two minutes, you do it at least weekly, the steps do not change much, and the output looks roughly the same every time.

Real candidates: weekly status reports, meeting prep, invoice processing, content repurposing, email triage, morning briefings. Write it down in one sentence: *Every [timeframe], I have to [task]. It takes about [time].*

**Good targets:** pattern-based, frequent, time-consuming. **Bad targets:** anything that needs nuanced judgment, anything low-frequency, anything that spends money or sends external messages without review. The best automation target is not the easiest task to automate. It is the one that saves the most time.

**The hidden cost of repetitive work.** Repetitive tasks do not just steal time — they steal attention. Every time you switch into manual mode to write the same report or format the same data, you pay a context-switching cost on top of the actual work. Automations that save thirty minutes of calendar time often save ninety minutes of real cognitive capacity because you stop dreading the task, stop context-switching into it, and stop carrying the mental overhead of tracking when it is due.

**How to find your best target.** Spend five minutes listing every recurring task from the past two weeks. Include small ones. Then score each on two axes: time cost (how long does it actually take) and misery cost (how much do you dislike doing it). The task with the highest combined score is your target. High misery tasks are automation-worthy even when the time savings are moderate — you get the hours back and you stop carrying the dread.

**Testing your target against the four criteria.** Take your top candidate and check each criterion. Can you explain it in two minutes? Try explaining it out loud right now. If you get confused or if the explanation keeps branching, the task has hidden complexity you have not mapped yet. Is it weekly? Monthly tasks still save time but make the automation harder to debug because you run it infrequently. Are the steps stable? If the process changes every time, start with a simpler version that only handles the common case. Does the output look consistent? Pull three examples of past output and compare them — if they look structurally similar, you are ready.

**The prompt to tell Claude what you picked.** Open Claude and paste: *I am building a workflow automation. Every [timeframe] I have to [task]. It takes me about [time]. The steps are roughly: [numbered list]. The output is: [describe what you produce]. I want you to help me build a master prompt that automates the middle of this workflow. We will map it first before building anything.*
steps.done_when: You have one sentence describing your automation target, plus a rough estimate of time spent on it per week.

steps.title: Map the workflow on paper
steps.body:
Every workflow has three parts: **Trigger** (what kicks it off), **Process** (the steps in the middle), and **Output** (the finished product). Before you open Claude, list every step end to end.

For each step, mark one of three tags: *Claude handles it*, *I handle it*, or *shared*. A first automation almost always looks like manual input, automated middle, human review on the way out.

**Minimize handoff points.** One handoff in (you paste raw data), one handoff out (you review and send). Anything more and the automation stops feeling automatic.

Even partial automation is enormous. If you paste data for 5 minutes and Claude writes and formats for 30 seconds, you just saved 40 minutes of work.

**How to map the trigger.** The trigger is whatever starts the workflow. It could be a calendar event (Friday at 4pm), an arriving document (a client sends you their monthly report), an inbox state (more than ten unread emails from the same sender), or a manual decision (you decide it is time to run the week's content). Write it explicitly. The trigger definition tells you what format your input data will come in — which determines what you will paste into Claude.

**How to map the process.** List every step in order. Be granular. "Write the report" is not a step — it is ten steps: read the raw data, identify the key metrics, calculate the changes from last period, identify the biggest win, identify the biggest risk, write the executive summary, write the section headers, fill each section, add formatting, proofread. Each granular step can be assigned ownership individually. Vague steps get owned by humans by default because Claude cannot act on ambiguity.

**How to assign ownership.** For each step: does it require accessing something only you can reach (a private system, a physical document, a conversation that happened verbally)? If yes, that step is yours. Does it require judgment that depends on relationship context Claude does not have (how much to emphasize something because you know the reader hates bad news)? If yes, that step is shared. Everything else is Claude's.

**The workflow map prompt.** After you have written your steps, paste them into Claude: *Here is my workflow map. Trigger: [trigger]. Steps: [numbered list with ownership tags]. Output: [description]. Review this and tell me: (1) are there steps I marked as mine that you could actually handle if I gave you the right context? (2) are there steps I marked as Claude's that will actually need human judgment I have not accounted for? (3) what is the minimum input I need to provide to hand off the maximum amount of work?*

**What a good handoff structure looks like.** Ideal automation: you paste one block of raw data (a spreadsheet export, a list of email subjects and senders, the key numbers from a dashboard). Claude processes it completely. You read and approve. You send. Two human touchpoints, zero repetitive thinking in between.
steps.done_when: You have a numbered list of every step from trigger to output, with ownership marked for each step.

steps.title: Set up a dedicated workspace
steps.body:
Open Claude and create a **Project** dedicated to this automation. Name it clearly (e.g. *Weekly Status Report*).

In custom instructions, give Claude the context it needs every run: what the task is, who the output is for, what tone it should use, and any hard rules. Upload reference material: templates, a previous example output, your style guide, or an audience description.

Each automated step probably needs at least one capability: reading documents, doing math, formatting output, drafting copy. **Test each piece in isolation** before you chain them. Can Claude actually read the format your input comes in? Does the formatting match your template? Debugging a single step is 10x easier than debugging a full chain.

**What to put in custom instructions.** Write the custom instructions in four sections. Section one: task description. *This project automates [your workflow]. Every [timeframe] I paste [description of raw input] and you produce [description of output].* Section two: audience and tone. *The output goes to [who]. They care about [what]. Tone: [specific description]. Never: [things to avoid].* Section three: format rules. *Output must follow this template: [paste your template or link to uploaded version]. Required sections: [list]. Section lengths: [constraints].* Section four: edge cases. *If the input data is missing [field], note it explicitly rather than estimating. If a metric is unusually high or low (more than 20% change), flag it with a bold WARNING.*

**What to upload.** Upload three things: a real example of your raw input (the data you paste in), a real example of your best previous output, and any template you want Claude to follow. If the output is an email, upload a past email that performed well. If it is a slide deck, upload the slide template. If it is a spreadsheet, upload the spreadsheet with the rows you fill in manually highlighted.

**How to test capabilities in isolation.** Before building the master prompt, test each step independently. For a status report: paste raw data and ask Claude to extract the top five metrics. Does it get them right? Paste those metrics and ask Claude to write the executive summary. Does it match your voice? Paste the summary and ask Claude to format it in your email template. Does the structure hold? Only chain steps together after each one passes individually.

**The isolation test prompt.** For each step: *Test this specific capability in isolation. Here is the input: [paste sample]. The expected output for this step only is: [description]. Produce the output for this step only and tell me what was unclear in the input.*
steps.done_when: You have a dedicated Claude Project with custom instructions, reference materials uploaded, and each individual capability tested against real data.

steps.title: Build the master prompt
steps.body:
Now combine the steps into a single **master prompt**. Structure: trigger (*When I paste data below...*), numbered steps in order, output requirements, and any flagging logic.

Be specific about constraints that matter. *Executive summary must be exactly 3 sentences. Lead with the biggest win, then the biggest risk, then the outlook.* Generic prompts produce generic output. Precise prompts produce precise output.

Add intelligence, not just formatting. *Flag any client with revenue down more than 10%.* *Surface any invoice over $5,000 for extra review.* This is where automation stops feeling like mail merge and starts feeling like a teammate.

Run the full flow with real data. Watch for: correct formatting, accurate numbers, matching tone, appropriate flags. Expect three or four iterations before it is tight.

**The master prompt template.** Use this structure as your starting point and fill in each bracket: *When I paste data below this line, do the following steps in order. [Step 1: what to extract or calculate]. [Step 2: what to analyze or compare]. [Step 3: what to write and in what format]. [Step 4: what to flag for special attention]. Output requirements: [format details]. Tone: [voice description]. Length constraints: [per-section word or sentence counts]. Always do: [non-negotiables]. Never do: [hard rules]. Here is the data: [PASTE HERE]*

**The difference between formatting and intelligence.** Formatting says: arrange the data in this structure. Intelligence says: interpret the data and surface what matters. Both are valuable, but intelligence is where Claude earns its place in your workflow. Pure formatting can be done with a spreadsheet template. What cannot be done with a template: spotting the anomaly in a dataset, identifying the risk buried in the third row of a table, writing a summary that emphasizes the right things given who is reading it. Build intelligence into your master prompt with specific instructions: *If any metric changed more than 15% week over week, bold the number and add a one-sentence explanation of what likely drove the change.*

**How to iterate toward tight output.** Run the master prompt with real data from a past week where you already know what the right output looks like. Compare Claude's output to your previous best version. Make a list: what is different? For each difference, decide: is this a prompt problem (Claude did not have the right instruction) or a judgment call (Claude made a defensible choice that you would have made differently)? Fix only the prompt problems. Judgment calls that are close are acceptable — you will review before sending anyway.

**The iteration prompt.** After each test run: *Compare your output to this reference example: [paste your best previous output]. List every difference. For each difference, tell me whether it is a format issue, a tone issue, a content issue, or a judgment call. Then suggest the specific change to the master prompt that would fix each format, tone, and content issue.*
steps.done_when: Your master prompt runs end to end on real data and produces output you would actually use.

steps.title: Harden it and save the prompt
steps.body:
Recurring output problems mean the prompt needs updating. Fix the source, not the output. If the summary is always too long, add a length constraint to the prompt. If the hook is always weak, add an example of a hook that worked.

Keep a **human review checkpoint** before anything external gets sent. Claude drafts, you scan, you fix one thing if needed, you send. Ninety-five percent time savings with zero blast radius.

Save your master prompt somewhere you will find it again: the Project description, a README, a note pinned in your notes app, or a shell alias. A one-off conversation gets lost. A saved prompt is infrastructure.

When requirements change, update the prompt. Add a step, tweak a constraint, upload a new reference doc. The framework holds. This is a living system now.

**How to identify what needs hardening.** Run the automation three times with different real-data inputs. After each run, mark every place you had to manually edit the output before it was ready to send. Collect all the edits across three runs. Group them by type: tone edits, length edits, missing information edits, format edits. The type with the most edits is where the prompt needs the most work. Fix the highest-frequency edit type first.

**The hardening prompt.** Tell Claude: *I have run this automation three times. Here are the edits I had to make each time: [paste your list]. For each pattern of edits, tell me what instruction is missing or too vague in the master prompt and give me the exact revised instruction to add.* Claude will translate your editing patterns into prompt fixes. Test the updated prompt on one of the same inputs you edited manually. The edit should no longer be necessary.

**The human review checkpoint.** Design your automation so the final human step is a scan, not a rewrite. If you are regularly rewriting more than one sentence before sending, the automation is not tight enough yet. If you are scanning and occasionally tweaking a word or two, the automation is working correctly. The difference is in the prompt precision. Keep iterating until review is a scan.

**How to save the master prompt.** Paste the final version into the Project description in Claude — this is the first place you will look for it when you come back in three weeks. Also copy it into a note, a gist, or a dedicated automation doc. Name the file or note clearly: *[Task] Master Prompt — Updated [Date].* Include the date because prompts evolve and you want to know which version is current.

**Building a prompt changelog.** Keep a short log of what you changed and why. One line per change: *[Date]: Added 15% threshold flag after three runs showed I was manually adding it every time. Removed "always include a quote" rule because the quotes were consistently weak.* A changelog prevents you from re-introducing instructions you already tried and rejected, and it makes it easy to roll back a change if a new instruction creates a different problem.
steps.done_when: Your master prompt is saved somewhere reusable and you have run it at least once against real data for this week's actual work.

steps.title: Ship it
steps.body:
Write a one or two sentence description of your automation: *what it does, how long it used to take, how long it takes now.* Optionally paste a link to the master prompt (in a gist, a note, or a doc) or a screenshot of a before-and-after.

You did not just automate one task. You learned a framework: **map, set up, chain, test, save.** That same framework works for the next automation you build. And the next one takes half the time to build.

You used to do the work. Now you design the system that does the work.

**What you actually built.** You built a repeatable system: a trigger, a processing chain, and a human review gate. That structure is the backbone of every professional automation — from the spreadsheet macros accountants have used for decades to the AI-powered pipelines running at scale companies today. The inputs change, the tools change, the complexity scales, but the structure does not: data in, rules applied, output reviewed, output sent.

**What to automate next.** You now have a workflow map for one task and a master prompt that runs it. The next automation will take half as long to build because you already know the framework. Look at your list from Step 1 — you scored every recurring task. Move to the second-highest scorer. Apply the same process: map it, set up a Project, test capabilities in isolation, build the master prompt, harden it, save it.

**How to measure what you saved.** Before you close this project, calculate the real number. Take the time you used to spend on this task per week and multiply by 52. That is the annual time you reclaimed. For a forty-five-minute weekly report: 45 minutes x 52 weeks = 39 hours per year. That is a full work week. Write that number down. It is the concrete case you make when you tell someone why you spent an afternoon building an automation.

**The prompt to share with a colleague.** If someone in your team does the same task manually, paste your master prompt to them with this note: *Paste your data at the bottom and it runs itself. I automated [task] — it used to take me [old time], now it takes [new time]. Took about [hours] to build. Happy to walk you through it if you want to build one for your version.* Automations spread person to person. You are now the person who knows how.
steps.done_when: You have your description and optional link ready to submit.
